title: The Pillay Family Trail: Pilgrims of St Martha's Hill
area: The Chantries & St Martha's Hill, Guildford
blurb: Become little pilgrims! Follow the secret acorn signs up through shady Guildford pine woods to a tiny 900-year-old church right on top of the hill. Isaac cracks the clues, Eli's got a spotting mission at every stop. Mostly flat woodland with one gentle sandy climb — a comfy ~80–90 min that flexes shorter (just turn back at the church). Perfect for a hot day: tree cover nearly the whole way.
difficulty: family · ages 2 & 6 · shaded woods + one gentle hill · flexes 60–120 min

start.name: St Martha's Hill car park, Halfpenny Lane (GU4 8PZ · W3W shady.alien.cheek)
start.directions: ~10 min drive from Guildford to the free St Martha's Hill car park on Halfpenny Lane (GU4 8PZ; What3Words shady.alien.cheek). Out 8:30, easily home for 10:30. The path is mostly flat, SHADED woodland up toward the hilltop church (~15 min each way), then a short sandy climb to the top. Buggy-friendly through the woods; the final sandy pull to the church may need a carry or sling for Eli. ⚠️ No loos or café at the car park — go before you set off, ice cream back in Guildford after. Heavy tree cover means phone GPS can wander, so use the “GPS playing up? I'm here” button if a clue won't unlock.

stops.name: Follow the Acorn
stops.clue: Welcome, pilgrims! For hundreds of years walkers have followed this path over the hills. Find a wooden signpost for the “North Downs Way” — carved on it is a little nut that grows on big oak trees. What nut is it?
stops.answer_hint: Squirrels bury them for winter, and they grow into mighty oak trees…
stops.little_ones: Eli — can you find an acorn or an oak leaf on the ground, then give the biggest tree a great big hug? 🌳
stops.grownup: The acorn is the waymark for ALL of Britain's National Trails. The North Downs Way (opened 1978) runs right over St Martha's Hill, following the medieval Pilgrims' Way towards Canterbury.
stops.story: This path is part of the old Pilgrims' Way — the route people walked all the way to Canterbury. Spot the little acorn signs and you're walking in their footsteps.

stops.name: The Whispering Pines
stops.clue: These woods are called The Chantries. Look UP — they're full of tall trees that stay green all year long and drop hard, scaly cones. What kind of tree is that: pine, oak, or palm?
stops.answer_hint: Not an oak (no acorns up there!) and definitely not a palm — think Christmas-tree shape.
stops.little_ones: Eli — hunt for a pine cone on the ground, then close your eyes and listen to the wind in the treetops. Sshh… 🌲
stops.grownup: The Chantries: ~200 acres of pine, conifer and sweet chestnut owned by Guildford. A 'chantry' was money left in medieval times to pay priests to sing prayers for someone's soul.
stops.story: The Chantries are packed with tall pines and chestnut trees — lovely and shady on a hot day. The name comes from 'chantry': money left long ago so priests would sing prayers up on the hill.

stops.name: The Pilgrims' Sandy Climb
stops.clue: Now the path turns soft and SANDY and starts to climb — you're nearly at the top! Real pilgrims walked hundreds of miles to reach this hill. March like a pilgrim up to this spot and check in.
stops.little_ones: Eli — the path is sandy like a beach! Can you stamp your feet and count your big pilgrim steps? One, two, three… 👣
stops.grownup: St Martha's Hill is soft Greensand, not the chalk found across much of the North Downs — that's why the summit paths feel sandy and golden underfoot.
stops.story: Almost there! This sandy path is the final climb pilgrims made up to the church — sandy because the whole hill is made of soft golden Greensand.

stops.name: The Secret Hilltop Church
stops.clue: Right on top of the hill stands a tiny ancient stone church — St Martha's. There's been a church here for nearly 900 years! Look closely at the old stone arches and windows: a pattern is carved all the way round in sharp points, like a row of little mountains. What shape is it — zig-zags, circles, or squares?
stops.answer_hint: Up, down, up, down — like the teeth of a saw, or lots of letter 'V's in a row.
stops.little_ones: Eli — can you find the big old wooden door and give it a gentle knock, then point right up at the pointy church tower? ⛪
stops.grownup: St Martha's-on-the-Hill — a 12th-C Norman church, rebuilt 1848–50 by 32-year-old architect Henry Woodyer for £700 and re-consecrated on 15 May 1850. The 'zig-zag' is Norman chevron moulding, kept from the original medieval church.
stops.story: St Martha's is nearly 900 years old. That zig-zag carving is called 'Norman chevron' — builders loved it 900 years ago and it's still here today. Pilgrims rested at this very church on the long walk to Canterbury.

stops.name: Top of the World
stops.clue: Stand by the church and look OUT — you're 175 metres up, one of the highest spots for miles around! On a clear day the view stretches right across the Surrey Hills. Find the best view and check in, champion pilgrims.
stops.little_ones: Eli — do your BIGGEST wave to everyone down in Guildford, and see if you can spot a bird flying below you! 👋
stops.grownup: The summit is 574 ft (175 m). On a clear day the view reaches across the North Downs and Surrey Hills — some say as far as the South Downs.
stops.story: You made it to the very top! One of the highest points around — pilgrims would have stopped right here to catch their breath and take in the view, just like you.

finale.title: Pilgrims Isaac & Eli, you conquered St Martha's Hill! ⛪🎉
finale.message: You followed the acorn, found the whispering pines, climbed the sandy path, discovered the 900-year-old zig-zag church and reached the top of the world. Proper little pilgrims! Show Mum and Dad all your stickers.
finale.reward_idea: An ice cream back down in Guildford — pilgrims' orders! 🍦

credits: Map data © OpenStreetMap contributors · facts © Wikipedia (CC BY-SA) / Historic England / North Downs Way
_recce_notes: PREP DRAFT 2026-06-19 for Sat 20 Jun AM (8:30–10:30 door-to-door window). Start = St Martha's Hill car park, Halfpenny Lane GU4 8PZ (W3W shady.alien.cheek). Route: shaded Chantry/St Martha's woodland track up to the summit church (~900m each way, ~15 min per The Ambling Path), mostly flat then a gentle sandy climb; ~3 km / ~80–90 min with the boys, flexes shorter (turn back at the church). HIGH-confidence pin: St Martha's church (stop 4) ~51.2194,-0.5436 — fixed landmark. SOFT pins to FOOT-VERIFY + retune geofences on the walk: car park exact entrance (postcode centroid 51.2276,-0.5380), stop 1 acorn/NDW signpost, stop 2 pines, stop 3 sandy-climb point, stop 5 viewpoint — all interpolated along the track; GPS is weak under canopy so geofences set generous (45–55 m) and the 'I'm here' override is available. BUGGY: woodland loop is pushchair-friendly (The Ambling Path) but the final sandy track to the church may need a carry/sling for Eli — verify. Durable clues confirmed by research: NDW acorn waymark (stop 1) + Norman zig-zag/chevron carving on the church (stop 4) — confirm the zig-zag is on an arch/window visible at child height.
